exemplar.title: My aim with this investigation is to find the route that is the shortest in total time to interrail around the 10 chosen cities, and find how much of Europe that route brings us through.

detailedExemplarData.title: My aim with this investigation is to find the route that is the shortest in total time to interrail around the 10 chosen cities, and find how much of Europe that route brings us through.

subject.title: Mathematics Analysis and Approaches (AA)
subject.description:
**IB Mathematics Analysis and Approaches** is a **Group 5** subject in the **International Baccalaureate Diploma Programme (IBDP)**. It explores **number and algebra, functions, geometry and trigonometry, statistics and probability, and calculus**, with a strong focus on **theoretical understanding, formal mathematical reasoning, and analytical problem-solving**.

The course is available at **Standard Level (SL, 150 teaching hours)** and **Higher Level (HL, 240 teaching hours)**, with **first assessment in 2021**.

### Assessment at a Glance

| Assessment | Format | SL | HL | Weighting |
|------------|--------|----|----|-----------|
| **Paper 1** | Non-calculator written exam | 90 minutes | 120 minutes | 40% |
| **Paper 2** | Calculator allowed written exam | 90 minutes | 120 minutes | 40% |
| **Paper 3** | Calculator allowed problem-solving | N/A | 60 minutes | 20% (HL only) |
| **Mathematical Exploration** | Internal Assessment | 20 hours | 20 hours | 20% |

strengths: Clear, informative title and excellent use of headings/sub-headings to guide the reader
Exceptionally well organized with a textbook-like structure: introduction, body, conclusion, pagination, captions and appendix
Coherent narrative linking problem statement, methods, results and evaluation
weaknesses: Occasional verbosity and digressions in personal anecdotes disrupt flow
Some figures (e.g., low-resolution images, overlapping superscripts) hinder readability
Inconsistent footnote/citation formatting detracts from professional presentation
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: While minor lapses in conciseness and figure formatting prevent a perfect band-4 in every strand, the explorationâs structure is exceptionally clear, logically developed and easy to navigate. Headings, numbered figures, tables and appendix deliver an outstanding presentation overall, justifying the top mark in this criterion.

strengths: Consistent and accurate use of matrices, set notation and arrow notation in most places
Effective multiple representations: distance matrices, tables, network maps and triangle diagrams
Clear logical structure in algorithm descriptions (bounds â exact method â verification â extension)
weaknesses: Minor inconsistencies mixing h/min formats and decimal hours
Some figures are pixelated; more pseudocode or flowcharts would strengthen clarity
Occasional terminology slips (brute-force vs branch-and-bound) blur precision
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The exploration communicates mathematics with overall consistency and clarity, employing a variety of appropriate representations and maintaining logical progression. Minor notation and formatting issues keep it just below the highest descriptive ideal, but the work demonstrates the coherent, accurate mathematical communication characteristic of a band-4 performance.

strengths: Personal travel context shows significant independent thinking and initiative
Clear personal approach in framing the exploration around the studentâs own route-planning challenge
Good creativity in combining heuristic bounds with branch-and-bound and extending to surface-area estimation
weaknesses: Method choices (e.g., city selection, algorithms) are only briefly justified
Implementation often follows standard online examples rather than fully novel development
Personal voice is clear but innovation remains moderate rather than exceptional
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The student demonstrates significant independent thinking, a personal connection to the problem and a good level of creativity. However, methodological choices lack deep justification and the exploration does not exhibit the outstanding originality or highly innovative approach required for the top band.

strengths: Meaningful reflection on algorithmic limitations and discussion of bounds throughout
Thoughtful analysis comparing final route time with bounds and evaluating key assumptions
Clear evaluation of outcomes, including practical travel implications and suggestions for improvement
weaknesses: Reflection remains at a general level without deep quantification of errors
Limited exploration of alternative models or future research directions
Insights are thoughtful but do not reach the depth of substantial critical reflection
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The reflection is coherent and demonstrates a thoughtful analysis of results and methods, along with clear evaluation of outcomes. Nevertheless, it stops short of the deep, critical and forward-looking reflection needed for the top band, warranting a middle-band score.

strengths: Relevant mathematics (graph theory, heuristics, combinatorics, trigonometry) is applied effectively
Good understanding and mostly correct execution of calculations for MST, TSP bounds, factorial growth and area scaling
Integration of code and uniform units (minutes) strengthens the mathematical underpinning of the investigation
weaknesses: Minor inaccuracies in terminology (branch-and-bound vs brute-force) and rounding during unit conversions
Implementation does not fully align with described pruning method
Lack of formal notation in MST presentation and limited discussion of asymmetric travel effects
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The mathematics employed is fully commensurate with SL expectations and serves the investigation purpose coherently. Despite minor errors and missed opportunities for formalism or deeper analysis, the studentâs thorough understanding and largely correct application justify a solid band-4 score in this criterion.

strandSelections.reasoning:
Explanations follow a logical order: bounds â exact method â verification â area extension.  Each algorithm is introduced, applied and then discussed.  Occasional mathematical slips (e.g.
using a brute-force permutation loop while labelling it branch-and-bound) blur clarity slightly, so the strand earns a solid but not perfect score.

Calculations for total travel time, MST length and triangle areas are mostly correct, showing good understanding.  Minor inaccuracies (confusion between branch-and-bound and brute-force, possible rounding issues in minute-to-hour conversions) prevent a flawless score, but overall grasp is thorough and largely accurate.

The student employs tables of times, a distance matrix, labelled network maps, and triangle diagrams.  These are generally clear and support the mathematics effectively.  The variety is good, but some figures are pixelated and a graph of convergence or a coded output screenshot could have strengthened the representation set, so the work sits comfortably in the âeffectiveâ rather than âhighly effectiveâ band.

The exploration follows a clear narrative â introduction of the real-life problem, description of successive algorithms, presentation of results and a concluding evaluation.  Transitions between sections are largely smooth and the reader can see how each stage builds on the previous one.  Minor lapses (e.g. digressions about personal travel experience or repeated explanations of what a Hamiltonian cycle is) break the flow occasionally, preventing the work from reaching the âhighly coherent/excellentâ threshold.

Creativity is shown in extending the optimisation task to an estimated surface-area calculation, and in adapting MST by re-attaching Berlin.  Yet these ideas are moderate elaborations of established techniques rather than breakthroughs, placing the work in the âgood level of creativity and initiativeâ band.

Analyses include comparing the final time with upper/lower bounds and evaluating assumptions such as symmetric travel times and map flatness.  While thoughtful, the analysis does not probe alternative mathematical models or quantify potential errors, so it is judged âthoughtfulâ rather than âdeep and insightful.â

The conclusion relates the route time to practical travel plans and computes the percentage of the Schengen area covered.  Suggestions for improvement (wider city spread, additional exact algorithms) indicate a clear evaluation, but implications are not explored in great depth, warranting a band-2 award.

The student reflects on algorithmic limitations (local nature of nearest-neighbour, brute-force inefficiency, coding error sensitivity) throughout the discussion, demonstrating meaningful but not deep critical reflection.  Comments are insightful yet remain fairly general, matching the middle band descriptor.

Matrices, set notation, arrow notation for routes and standard algorithm symbols are used correctly in most places.  Some inconsistencies (mixing h/min with decimal hours; writing â$\theta$ Ã· 0.85â in the scale conversion) keep the communication just short of fully consistent and accurate.

The report is clearly framed around the studentâs own travel plans and includes personal commentary, demonstrating a distinct personal voice.  Nonetheless, the mathematical pathway largely mirrors common TSP tutorials, limiting the workâs uniqueness.  Hence a clear but not highly innovative personal approach is evident.

Almost all material included is relevant to solving the route-planning task; however, the narrative is quite wordy (e.g. repeated justification of adding two Berlin edges to the MST, extended travel anecdotes).  At 20 pages the piece is not strictly concise, so it fits the band in which content is "concise with mostly relevant content" rather than the highest descriptor.

All mathematics â factorial growth, MST, nearest neighbour heuristic, brute-force/branch-and-bound code, cosine rule and area scaling â directly serves the investigation goals.  The selection is richer than the minimum expected at SL and is applied effectively, but not so comprehensive as to merit the top two bands.

Choosing a personally meaningful travel competition and independently assembling real train-time data shows significant initiative.  Selecting and chaining three different algorithms evidences personal decision-making beyond textbook examples.  However, much of the implementation follows standard online resources, and critical choices (e.g. why 10 cities, why those specific algorithms) are only briefly justified, so the evidence aligns with âsignificantâ rather than âoutstandingâ independent thinking.

Headings, sub-headings, numbered figures and tables give the report a textbook-like structure.  The introduction, body (data, algorithms, calculations) and conclusion are easy to locate and follow.  Pagination, figure captions and an appendix further enhance navigation, matching the descriptor of an âexceptionally well organisedâ exploration.

Graph theory and basic trigonometry are comfortably at or slightly above SL level yet remain accessible.  The student integrates them appropriately and in context; however, implementation relies on internet-generated code rather than fully self-developed mathematics, so the standard is âfully commensurateâ but not âexpertly applied.â

annotations.comment: The title is clear and informative, immediately establishing the context and scope of the exploration. Excellent use of headings to guide the reader.
The introduction is engaging but overly verbose with personal anecdotes that digress from the mathematical focus. Consider condensing travel backstory to maintain coherence and relevance.
When reframing as a TSP, include the formal definition $$	ext{TSP}:	ext{ find a Hamiltonian cycle minimizing total cost}$$ and reference graph notation to strengthen mathematical clarity.
Good use of the factorial expression $$9!$$ to illustrate route growth. You might add a brief derivation of $$ (n-1)! $$ for clarity when defining the number of permutations.

The description of upper and lower bounds is conceptually accurate but the lower bound definition (âminimum solution but not viableâ) is confusing. Clarify its relationship to the MST and Hamiltonian cycles.
The code in the appendix uses brute-force (permutations) rather than pruning by bounds, contradicting the branch-and-bound description. Align the implementation with the chosen method.
Consider adding pseudocode or flowchart for the nearest neighbour heuristic to enhance clarity and demonstrate a logical structure for the algorithmâs stepwise decisions.
The explanation of Kruskalâs MST is sound, but formal notation for edges (e.g., $$E$$, $$V$$, weights $$w(e)$$) would strengthen the mathematical communication.
The description of branch-and-bound occasionally conflates brute-force and pruning techniques. Ensure terminology is precise, distinguishing exhaustive search from bound-based elimination.
Figure 1 is clear but low resolution. Improving image quality or using vector graphics will enhance readability and professionalism of the data presentation.
You assume symmetry of travel times without quantifying potential error. A brief discussion of how asymmetry could affect results would demonstrate deeper critical analysis.
Figure 2âs table is dense and mixing hours/min with HTML breaks reduces clarity. Converting all times to decimal hours or separate columns for hours and minutes would improve readability.
Footnotes formatting is inconsistent: some URLs inline, others broken. Adopt a consistent citation style (e.g., APA) for sources and ensure uniformity in formatting.
The narrative describing the nearest neighbour process is well structured and shows clear logical progression through each step of the algorithm.
When listing the route, explicitly show the total time calculation to connect the description with the computed upper bound of 63 h 52 min, reinforcing consistency.
Figure 3 lacks a legend or annotation key for coloured nodes. Including a clear legend will help interpret the upper bound graph more effectively.
The narrative around the Hamiltonian graph in Figure 4 is broken across paragraphs, making it harder to follow. Consolidate and reference the figure more directly in text.

The step-by-step summation $$3h46 + \,

each$$ is correct, but mixing total minutes (352 min) within the same line is confusing. Separate the conversion $$ rac{352}{60}$$ for clarity.
The heuristic-to-exact workflow demonstrates initiative in combining bounds with branch-and-bound. Well justified choice of methods.
Figure 5âs edge ordering table uses superscript edge numbers that overlap, making it hard to read. Reformat the table or separate columns for clarity.
The combination of heuristic bounds and branch-and-bound reveals good understanding and independent decision-making in method selection.
The MST length calculation shows the sum of weighted edges, but lacks the intermediate minuteâtoâhour conversion step (e.g., $$344 \,	ext{min}=5h44min$$). Include it for completeness.
The reflection on the lower boundâs limitations is insightful but could be deepened by quantifying how far off it lies from the optimal route time.
The bound interval $$45h35min863h52min$$ is clearly stated in equation form, effectively framing the exact search space.
The uniform distance matrix in minutes is a strong choice for code input, showing good application of uniform units.
